questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.section: 4.4 The Major Trends in the Periodic Table
questions.question: The first electron affinity of chlorine is -349 kJ/mol. What does the negative sign indicate?

options.A: Energy is absorbed when chlorine gains an electron.
options.B: Energy is released when chlorine gains an electron.
options.C: Chlorine has a positive charge after gaining an electron.

questions.hint: Negative sign in thermodynamics usually indicates exothermic (energy released).
questions.explanation: A negative electron affinity means energy is released when the electron is added. Chlorine atom gains an electron exothermically, forming Cl⁻. Option A describes positive EA; C and D are incorrect.

calculation_steps: Negative sign in EA = exothermic = energy released.

source_trace.section_anchor: 4.4 The Major Trends in the Periodic Table
source_trace.content_anchor: First electron affinities are negative ... energy is released

explanations_tiered.definition: A negative electron affinity means the process of adding an electron is exothermic (releases energy).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For chlorine, Cl(g) + e⁻ → Cl⁻(g) releases 349 kJ/mol, indicated by the negative sign.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Negative means energy is given off, like heat from a fire. Chlorine 'feels good' when it gains an electron and releases energy.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In thermochemistry, negative ΔE indicates an exothermic reaction (releases energy). Chlorine's electron affinity is negative because the added electron is strongly attracted to the nucleus, releasing energy.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The negative sign convention for electron affinity follows thermodynamics: exothermic processes have negative ΔH (or ΔE). Cl has a more exothermic EA than F due to lower electron repulsion in the larger 3p orbital.
worked_solution: Step 1: Recall that electron affinity is the energy change for X(g) + e⁻ → X⁻(g).
Step 2: Negative ΔE means energy is released (exothermic).
Step 3: For Cl, ΔE = -349 kJ/mol, so energy is released.
reasoning_steps: Interpret negative sign in thermodynamic context.
Relate to exothermic vs endothermic.
Apply to chlorine's EA.
misconceptions: Negative does not mean 'bad' or 'less'; it means energy released.

glossary_entry.definition: Negative value indicates exothermic process (energy released) when an electron is added.

flashcard.front: What does a negative electron affinity value mean?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Energy is released when the atom gains an electron (exothermic).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Energy released when atom gains an electron (exothermic).
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Negative sign indicates exothermic (energy released).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.section: 4.4 The Major Trends in the Periodic Table
questions.question: Using the Pauling scale, which of the following pairs of elements has the largest electronegativity difference?
questions.hint: Recall approximate Pauling values: Na ~0.9, Cl ~3.0; F ~4.0, I ~2.5; O ~3.5, H ~2.1; C ~2.5, H ~2.1.
questions.explanation: Na (0.9) and Cl (3.0) have a difference of 2.1. Other pairs: F and I (4.0 - 2.5 = 1.5), H and O (3.5 - 2.1 = 1.4), C and H (2.5 - 2.1 = 0.4). Thus Na-Cl has the largest difference, leading to ionic character.

source_trace.section_anchor: 4.4 The Major Trends in the Periodic Table
source_trace.content_anchor: fluorine is the most electronegative element with a value of 4.0 and cesium is the least electronegative element with a value of 0.7

explanations_tiered.definition: Electronegativity difference is the absolute difference between two atoms' Pauling electronegativities; larger differences indicate more ionic bonds.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Na (0.9) and Cl (3.0) have a difference of 2.1, the largest among the given pairs, explaining the ionic nature of NaCl.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Na is a metal (low pull), Cl is a nonmetal (high pull). The difference in their 'electron tugging' is very large.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Pauling values: Na=0.9, Cl=3.0 (Δ=2.1); F=4.0, I=2.5 (Δ=1.5); O=3.5, H=2.1 (Δ=1.4); C=2.5, H=2.1 (Δ=0.4). Na-Cl has the largest Δ, meaning the most ionic bond.
explanations_tiered.advanced: A ΔEN > 1.7 generally indicates ionic bonding. Na-Cl (2.1) is ionic; F-I (1.5) is polar covalent; H-O (1.4) is polar covalent; C-H (0.4) is nonpolar covalent.
worked_solution: Step 1: Recall Pauling electronegativity values.
Step 2: Compute differences: Na-Cl 2.1, F-I 1.5, H-O 1.4, C-H 0.4.
Step 3: Largest difference = Na-Cl (2.1).
reasoning_steps: Memorize common Pauling values.
Calculate ΔEN for each pair.
Compare and select largest.
misconceptions: Largest electronegativity difference does not always mean most ionic if one element is not a metal, but Na-Cl qualifies.

glossary_entry.definition: Absolute difference in electronegativity between two atoms; predicts bond polarity.

flashcard.front: Which pair has the largest electronegativity difference: Na-Cl, F-I, H-O, C-H?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which has larger ΔEN: Na-Cl or F-I?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Difference in EN values; predicts bond type.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Remind students that F is most electronegative, but difference with I is only 1.5.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. ΔEN values: Na-Cl 2.1, F-I 1.5, H-O 1.4, C-H 0.4.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.section: 4.4 The Major Trends in the Periodic Table
questions.question: Which of the following statements correctly explains why the second ionization energy of an element is always higher than its first ionization energy?

options.A: The atom becomes smaller after losing the first electron.
options.B: The remaining electrons are held more tightly because the nuclear charge remains the same but there are fewer electrons.
options.C: The atom gains mass after losing an electron.
options.D: The second electron is always in a lower principal energy level.

questions.hint: After losing one electron, the ion is positively charged, increasing attraction on remaining electrons.
questions.explanation: After the first electron is removed, the ion has a net positive charge, so the remaining electrons experience a stronger effective nuclear charge and are held more tightly. Option A is not the primary reason; C false; D not always true.

source_trace.section_anchor: 4.4 The Major Trends in the Periodic Table
source_trace.content_anchor: Each successive electron requires more energy to be released. This is because after the first electron is lost, the overall charge of the atom becomes positive, and the negative forces of the electron will be attracted to the positive charge

explanations_tiered.definition: Second ionization energy is higher than the first because the positive ion attracts remaining electrons more strongly than the neutral atom did.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: After losing an electron, the ion has a positive charge, increasing the effective nuclear charge felt by remaining electrons.
explanations_tiered.beginner: After losing one electron, the atom is positive, so it holds onto its other electrons tighter. It takes more energy to remove the next one.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: IE₂ > IE₁ because the ion X⁺ has a higher charge-to-electron ratio than neutral X. The remaining electrons are pulled more strongly by the unchanged nucleus, with less electron-electron repulsion.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The increase in successive ionization energies is described by Coulomb's law: the force of attraction on an electron is proportional to (Z_eff)/r². After removing an e⁻, Z_eff increases significantly, so IE₂ >> IE₁.
reasoning_steps: Recall that IE₁ removes the first electron from a neutral atom.
After removal, X⁺ has a positive charge.
Positive charge attracts remaining electrons more strongly, requiring more energy to remove another.
misconceptions: The atom does not become smaller enough to explain the large jump; the key is the net positive charge.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: IE₂ > IE₁ because X⁺ attracts electrons more strongly.

glossary_entry.definition: IE increases with each electron removed because the ion becomes more positively charged, holding remaining electrons more tightly.

flashcard.front: Why is second ionization energy always larger than first?
flashcard.flashcard_back: After first electron removal, the ion is positively charged, attracting remaining electrons more strongly.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: The ion X⁺ has a net positive charge, attracting remaining electrons more strongly.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: IE increases with each removal due to increasing positive charge.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: IE₂ > IE₁ due to positive ion attraction.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use Mg as example: IE₁ = 738, IE₂ = 1451 kJ/mol.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Positive charge after first removal increases attraction on remaining electrons.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.section: 4.4 The Major Trends in the Periodic Table
questions.question: Compare the atomic radii of Li (152 pm), Na (186 pm), and K (227 pm). What trend do these values illustrate and why?

options.A: Radii decrease down the group because nuclear charge increases.
options.B: Radii increase down the group because new electron shells are added, increasing distance from nucleus.
options.C: Radii remain constant down the group because shielding cancels nuclear charge.
options.D: Radii increase down the group because the number of protons decreases.

questions.hint: Look at the numbers: 152, 186, 227 — they are increasing.
questions.explanation: Down Group IA, atomic radius increases because each element has an additional electron shell (higher n), making the outermost electrons farther from the nucleus. Option A is opposite trend; C false; D false (protons increase).

calculation_steps: Values increase: Li 152 → Na 186 → K 227 pm.
Down group, new shell added each step.
Distance from nucleus increases, so radius increases.

source_trace.section_anchor: 4.4 The Major Trends in the Periodic Table
source_trace.content_anchor: atomic radius increases with increasing atomic number. orbital size increases with the increasing principal quantum number n

explanations_tiered.definition: Down a group, atomic radius increases because each successive element has an additional electron shell (higher principal quantum number).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Li (n=2), Na (n=3), K (n=4): each step adds a shell, placing valence electrons farther from the nucleus, increasing radius.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Going down the column, you add a new layer of electrons. More layers mean a bigger atom.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Down Group IA: Li (1s²2s¹), Na ([Ne]3s¹), K ([Ar]4s¹). The principal quantum number n increases, so the average distance of the valence electron from the nucleus increases, leading to larger atomic radius.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Despite increasing nuclear charge, the addition of a new principal energy level (higher n) dominates the size trend. The radial probability distribution shifts outward, as described by the Bohr model and quantum mechanics.
worked_solution: Step 1: Observe radii: Li 152, Na 186, K 227 pm.
Step 2: Recognise all are in Group IA.
Step 3: Down the group, n increases (2,3,4), so radius increases.
reasoning_steps: Identify the group (Group IA).
Recall trend down group: radius increases.
Explain using added shells (higher n).
misconceptions: Increased nuclear charge does not make atoms smaller down a group; the effect of added shells outweighs it.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Down group: n↑ → radius↑. Li < Na < K.

glossary_entry.definition: Atomic radius increases down a group due to addition of electron shells (higher n).

flashcard.front: Why does atomic radius increase from Li to K?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Each step adds a new electron shell (n increases), making the atom larger.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the trend in atomic radius down Group IA?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Radius increases (Li < Na < K) due to added shells.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Increases down group due to higher n.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Down group: more shells → larger radius.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use Li (152), Na (186), K (227) as concrete data.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe atomic radius trend down group
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Adding shells increases distance from nucleus, so radius increases.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.section: 4.4 The Major Trends in the Periodic Table
questions.question: Under what condition would you expect a very large jump between the second and third ionization energies of an element?

options.A: When the element is a noble gas.
options.B: When the third electron is removed from a stable noble gas core configuration.
options.C: When the element is in Group IA.
options.D: When the element is in Group VIIA.

questions.hint: A large jump occurs when you start removing electrons from a filled, stable inner shell.
questions.explanation: A large jump between IE₂ and IE₃ indicates that after removing two electrons, the ion has a stable noble gas configuration. Removing the next electron requires breaking into a filled shell, needing much more energy. Option B describes this. A, C, D are not specific enough.

source_trace.section_anchor: 4.4 The Major Trends in the Periodic Table
source_trace.content_anchor: Each successive electron requires more energy to be released

explanations_tiered.definition: A large jump in successive ionization energies occurs when removing an electron from a stable, filled noble gas core.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For example, Mg has IE₁=738, IE₂=1451, IE₃=7733 kJ/mol. The jump after IE₂ occurs because Mg²⁺ has a Ne configuration (1s²2s²2p⁶).
explanations_tiered.beginner: When an atom's ion becomes 'happy' with a full set of electrons (like a noble gas), removing another electron is extremely hard — that's when you see a big jump.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: A sharp increase in ionization energy signals the removal of an electron from a stable, filled subshell or noble gas core. Example: Al (IE₃ is much higher than IE₂ because Al²⁺ → Al³⁺ removes an electron from a filled 2p⁶ shell).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The jump is due to a dramatic increase in effective nuclear charge when the electron is removed from an inner shell. The energy required is an order of magnitude higher than removing valence electrons.
reasoning_steps: Recall that successive IEs increase gradually until a stable core is reached.
A large jump indicates transition from valence to core electron removal.
The core often has a noble gas configuration.
misconceptions: The jump is not after every electron; it occurs when you start removing core electrons.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Large IE jump = removing electron from stable noble gas core.

glossary_entry.definition: A sharp increase in successive IE when a core electron is removed from a stable noble gas configuration.

flashcard.front: When do you see a large jump in successive ionization energies?
flashcard.flashcard_back: When removing an electron from a stable noble gas core (filled shell).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What causes a large jump in successive IE?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Removing an electron from a stable noble gas core (filled shell).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Sharp increase when removing core electron from stable configuration.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Big jump = core electron from noble gas core.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use Mg as example: IE₁ 738, IE₂ 1451, IE₃ 7733 kJ/mol.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. A large jump occurs when the next electron must be removed from a stable noble gas core.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.section: 4.4 The Major Trends in the Periodic Table
questions.question: Why does electronegativity generally increase from left to right across a period?

options.A: Atomic radius increases, so the nucleus is further from bonding electrons.
options.B: Effective nuclear charge increases and atomic radius decreases, enhancing attraction for bonding electrons.
options.C: The number of valence electrons decreases, so each electron is more tightly held.
options.D: Metallic character increases, leading to stronger electron attraction.

questions.hint: Think about two factors: nuclear pull and distance.
questions.explanation: Left to right, nuclear charge increases without adding new shells, so effective nuclear charge increases and atomic radius decreases. Both factors increase the atom's ability to attract bonding electrons, raising electronegativity. Option A opposite; C false (valence electrons increase); D false (metallic character decreases).

source_trace.section_anchor: 4.4 The Major Trends in the Periodic Table
source_trace.content_anchor: As we move across a period from left to right the nuclear charge increases and the atomic size decreases, therefore the value of electronegativity increases

explanations_tiered.definition: Electronegativity increases across a period due to increasing effective nuclear charge (Z_eff) and decreasing atomic radius, which strengthens the attraction for bonding electrons.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: More protons in the nucleus with no new shells pulls the electron cloud inward, making the atom more electron-hungry in bonds.
explanations_tiered.beginner: As you move right, atoms get smaller and the nucleus gets stronger, so they 'grab' bonding electrons more tightly.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Across a period: Z increases → Z_eff increases; electrons added to same shell → atomic radius decreases. Both changes increase the electrostatic attraction felt by bonding electrons, so electronegativity rises.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Electronegativity is proportional to (Z_eff)/r². Across a period, Z_eff increases and r decreases, causing a sharp increase in EN from left (EN ~0.9) to right (EN ~4.0).
reasoning_steps: Recall that across period, nuclear charge increases.
Atomic radius decreases.
Smaller radius and higher Z_eff → stronger pull on bonding electrons → higher EN.
misconceptions: Atomic radius decreases across period, not increases; this is key to the trend.

glossary_entry.definition: EN increases left to right due to increased Z_eff and decreased atomic radius.

flashcard.front: Why does electronegativity increase left to right across a period?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Nuclear charge increases, radius decreases, so bonding electrons are more strongly attracted.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Explain the increase in EN across a period.
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Nuclear charge increases, radius decreases → stronger attraction for bonding electrons.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Increases across period due to higher Z_eff and smaller radius.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Across period: more protons, smaller atom → higher EN.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use Na (0.9) to Cl (3.0) as dramatic example.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Increased effective nuclear charge and decreased atomic radius enhance electron attraction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.section: 4.4 The Major Trends in the Periodic Table
questions.question: Which of the following elements has the highest electronegativity?
questions.hint: Fluorine is at the top right corner of the periodic table.
questions.explanation: Fluorine has the highest electronegativity (4.0 on Pauling scale). Oxygen is second (3.5), Chlorine (3.0), Nitrogen (3.0). Therefore F is correct.

calculation_steps: Pauling values: F=4.0 (highest), O=3.5, Cl=3.0, N=3.0.

source_trace.section_anchor: 4.4 The Major Trends in the Periodic Table
source_trace.content_anchor: fluorine is the most electronegative element with a value of 4.0

explanations_tiered.definition: Fluorine is the most electronegative element on the Pauling scale, with a value of 4.0.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Electronegativity increases up and to the right, so fluorine at top right has the highest value.
explanations_tiered.beginner: F is at the top right corner of the periodic table. That's the 'electron grabber' champion.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: On the Pauling scale, fluorine is assigned 4.0, the highest value. Oxygen is next with 3.5, then chlorine and nitrogen at 3.0.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The high electronegativity of fluorine results from its small atomic radius (high Z_eff) and high electron affinity (though not highest), making it extremely efficient at attracting bonding electrons.
worked_solution: Step 1: Recall periodic trend: EN increases up and right.
Step 2: F is top right of the periodic table (excluding noble gases).
Step 3: F has the highest EN (4.0).
reasoning_steps: Locate elements on periodic table.
Apply trend: EN highest at top right.
Identify F as the most electronegative.
misconceptions: Oxygen is second, not first. F is highest.

glossary_entry.definition: Fluorine (F) has the highest electronegativity of all elements (4.0 on Pauling scale).

flashcard.front: Which element has the highest electronegativity?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Fluorine (F), with a Pauling value of 4.0.

publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Fluorine has the highest Pauling electronegativity (4.0).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.section: 4.4 The Major Trends in the Periodic Table
questions.question: In a molecule of hydrogen fluoride (HF), why does fluorine carry a partial negative charge and hydrogen a partial positive charge?

options.A: Fluorine has a smaller atomic radius than hydrogen.
options.B: Fluorine has a higher electronegativity than hydrogen.
options.C: Hydrogen has a higher ionization energy than fluorine.
options.D: Fluorine has a lower electron affinity than hydrogen.

questions.hint: The more electronegative atom pulls shared electrons closer.
questions.explanation: Fluorine (EN=4.0) is much more electronegative than hydrogen (EN=2.1). In the H-F bond, fluorine attracts the bonding electrons more strongly, becoming partially negative (δ⁻) and hydrogen partially positive (δ⁺). Option A is true but not the direct reason; C and D are false/irrelevant.

calculation_steps: Higher EN atom (F) becomes partially negative.

source_trace.section_anchor: 4.4 The Major Trends in the Periodic Table
source_trace.content_anchor: The tendency of an atom in a molecule to attract the shared pair of electrons towards itself is known as electronegativity

explanations_tiered.definition: In a polar covalent bond, the atom with higher electronegativity pulls shared electrons toward itself, acquiring a partial negative charge.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Fluorine (4.0) is more electronegative than hydrogen (2.1), so the bonding electrons are closer to F, giving F a δ⁻ and H a δ⁺.
explanations_tiered.beginner: F is an electron hog; it pulls the shared electrons closer to itself, making itself slightly negative and H slightly positive.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Electronegativity difference (1.9) causes bond polarity. The more electronegative atom (F) attracts the bonding electron pair, becoming the negative pole (δ⁻).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The dipole moment of HF arises from the unequal sharing of electrons due to the large electronegativity difference. F's higher EN means its nucleus exerts greater pull on the bonding pair, polarizing the bond.
worked_solution: Step 1: Compare electronegativities: F=4.0, H=2.1.
Step 2: Higher EN atom attracts bonding electrons more.
Step 3: F becomes partially negative, H partially positive.
reasoning_steps: Recall that higher EN atom pulls bonding electrons.
F has higher EN than H.
Therefore F is δ⁻, H is δ⁺.
misconceptions: Partial charges are not full charges; they are due to unequal electron sharing, not electron transfer.

glossary_entry.definition: Unequal sharing of electrons due to electronegativity difference; more electronegative atom gets partial negative charge.

flashcard.front: In HF, which atom is partially negative and why?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Fluorine, because it has higher electronegativity (4.0 vs H 2.1).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why is fluorine partially negative in HF?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: F has higher electronegativity (4.0) than H (2.1), so it attracts bonding electrons more strongly.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Unequal sharing; higher EN atom gets δ⁻.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use this to introduce dipole moments and polar molecules.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Fluorine's higher electronegativity makes it the negative pole in HF.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.section: 4.4 The Major Trends in the Periodic Table
questions.question: Given the atomic radii: Al (143 pm), Si (118 pm), P (110 pm), S (103 pm). Which of the following correctly interprets the trend?

options.A: Radii increase from Al to S due to increased shielding.
options.B: Radii decrease from Al to S because effective nuclear charge increases across the period.
options.C: Radii decrease because the number of electron shells decreases.
options.D: Radii increase because the atoms become more metallic.

questions.hint: Across Period 3, radii decrease from left to right.
questions.explanation: Al to S shows decreasing atomic radius (143 → 103 pm). Across a period, effective nuclear charge increases as protons are added without new shells, pulling electrons inward. Option A is opposite; C false (shells remain n=3); D false (metallic character decreases, radius decreases).

calculation_steps: Across period, Z increases, same n=3 shell.
Higher Z_eff pulls electrons inward, decreasing radius.

source_trace.section_anchor: 4.4 The Major Trends in the Periodic Table
source_trace.content_anchor: atomic radius decreases as we move from left to right across a period

explanations_tiered.definition: Across a period, atomic radius decreases due to increasing effective nuclear charge pulling electrons closer.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Al to S: protons increase (13 to 16) while electrons stay in the n=3 shell, increasing Z_eff and shrinking the atom.
explanations_tiered.beginner: As you move from Al to S, the atoms get smaller because the nucleus gets stronger with more protons, squeezing the electron cloud.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Period 3: Al (13 p⁺, 143 pm), Si (14, 118), P (15, 110), S (16, 103). More protons, same shell → stronger pull → smaller radius.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The trend reflects the increase in effective nuclear charge (Z_eff) without a compensating increase in shielding (electrons added to same shell). Z_eff increases by about 0.6 per proton across Period 3, reducing the atomic radius.
worked_solution: Step 1: Observe radii decreasing: 143, 118, 110, 103.
Step 2: Note elements are in Period 3.
Step 3: Apply trend: across period, Z_eff increases, radius decreases.
reasoning_steps: Identify the period (Period 3).
Recall trend: radius decreases left to right.
Relate to increasing nuclear charge without new shells.
misconceptions: More electrons do not automatically mean larger atom if nuclear charge also increases in the same shell.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Period 3: Al > Si > P > S in radius.

glossary_entry.definition: Atomic radius decreases from Al to S due to increasing Z_eff.

flashcard.front: What is the trend in atomic radius from Al to S and why?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Radius decreases because effective nuclear charge increases across the period.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Explain the decreasing atomic radius from Al to S.
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Increasing effective nuclear charge with no new shells pulls electrons closer.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Decreases across period due to higher Z_eff.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Across period 3: radius Al > Si > P > S.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Compare Z_eff values: Al 4.07, Si 4.29, P 4.89, S 5.10.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Increased effective nuclear charge pulls electrons inward, decreasing radius.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.section: 4.4 The Major Trends in the Periodic Table
questions.question: Which of the following elements has the lowest first ionization energy?
questions.hint: Ionization energy decreases down a group.
questions.explanation: All are Group IA elements. Ionization energy decreases down the group, so Rb (bottom) has the lowest IE. Li has the highest, followed by Na, K, then Rb. Option D is correct.

source_trace.section_anchor: 4.4 The Major Trends in the Periodic Table
source_trace.content_anchor: ionization energy decreases from top to bottom within a group

explanations_tiered.definition: Ionization energy decreases down a group; thus Rb, being the lowest in Group IA among the options, has the lowest IE.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: IE trend down Group IA: Li (520) > Na (496) > K (419) > Rb (408) > Cs (376). Rb is lower than K, Na, Li.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Going down the column, atoms get bigger and it's easier to steal an electron. So Rb (near bottom) has the lowest IE.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Down Group IA, atomic radius increases and shielding increases, reducing the effective nuclear charge on the valence electron. Therefore, IE decreases: Li > Na > K > Rb > Cs. Rb has a lower IE than K, Na, and Li.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The trend is monotonic: each step down adds a new principal quantum number (n=2,3,4,5), increasing the distance and shielding, outweighing the increase in nuclear charge. Rb (n=5) has a very low IE (~408 kJ/mol) compared to Li (n=2, 520 kJ/mol).
worked_solution: Step 1: Identify the group (Group IA).
Step 2: Recall IE decreases down group.
Step 3: Rb is lowest among Li, Na, K, Rb.
Step 4: Therefore Rb has lowest IE.
reasoning_steps: List elements in order down Group IA: Li, Na, K, Rb.
Recall IE decreases from top to bottom.
Select the lowest (Rb).
misconceptions: The smallest atom (Li) has highest IE, not lowest. Rb (largest) has lowest IE.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Down Group IA: IE decreases. Rb has lower IE than K, Na, Li.

glossary_entry.definition: IE decreases from Li to Cs; Rb has lower IE than K.

flashcard.front: Which alkali metal has the lowest first ionization energy among Li, Na, K, Rb?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which has lower IE: K or Rb?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Decreases down group; Rb < K < Na < Li.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Rb has lowest IE among Li, Na, K, Rb.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Cs has even lower, but not in options.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Predict lowest ionization energy in group
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. IE decreases down Group IA, so Rb (lowest) has lowest IE.
